file_name: Mishra et al. - 2023 - Crow search algorithm with deep transfer learning driven skin lesion detection on dermoscopic images.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep transfer learning approach (CSADTL-SLD) combining GoogleNet for feature extraction, Crow Search Algorithm (CSA) for parameter tuning, and a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) classifier to detect and classify skin lesions (Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Nevus) in dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper introduces a novel technique (CSADTL-SLD) combining the Crow Search Algorithm with deep transfer learning for skin lesion detection. It details original experimental methods including data pre-processing, feature extraction using GoogleNet, parameter tuning via CSA, and classification using MLP. The study presents original performance validation results on a dataset of 2000 samples, comparing accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and other metrics against existing methods, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Crow search algorithm with deep transfer learning driven skin lesion detection on dermoscopic images
brief_summary: This study proposes the CSADTL-SLD technique, which combines the Crow Search Algorithm (CSA) with deep transfer learning to detect and classify skin lesions in dermoscopic images. The method utilizes a median filter for noise removal, GoogleNet for feature extraction, CSA for parameter tuning, and a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) for classification. The model was evaluated on a dataset of 2000 samples comprising Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Nevus classes. Performance validation using a 70:30 train-test split demonstrated an average accuracy of 94.24% and sensitivity of 84.70% on the training set. Comparative analysis showed that CSADTL-SLD outperformed existing methods such as MAFCNN-SCD, MobileNet, and DenseNet-169 in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. The results indicate that this approach offers significant enhancements in detection accuracy and robustness for automated skin lesion diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the dataset with 374 samples. Used in classification experiments with reported accuracy of 94.86% (training) and 96.00% (testing).

One of the three classes in the dataset with 1372 samples. Used in classification experiments with reported accuracy of 93.07% (training) and 93.00% (testing).

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into three discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Nevus. The model uses an MLP classifier to predict these lesion types. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-score, and MCC for each class.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic images used for skin lesion detection, containing 2000 total samples across three classes. The specific source or public repository name is not provided in the text, but it is described as a collection of images with sample visuals shown in Fig. 2.

use.training: Used for training the CSADTL-SLD model (70% of the dataset, i.e., 1400 samples).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper reports a 70:30 train-test split.
use.testing: Used for testing the CSADTL-SLD model (30% of the dataset, i.e., 600 samples).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-score, MCC) and visualizations (ROC, PR curves) without statistical significance testing.

Training Phase (70% split).metric: Per-class and Average Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, F-score, MCC
Training Phase (70% split).notes: Results indicate the CSADTL-SLD system effectively distinguishes between three classes. The model achieved an average accuracy of 94.24% and sensitivity of 84.70% on the training set.

Testing Phase (30% split).metric: Per-class and Average Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, F-score, MCC
Testing Phase (30% split).notes: The CSADTL-SLD approach demonstrated similar performance on the test set with an average accuracy of 94.22% and sensitivity of 84.10%. The system consistently achieved higher Precision-Recall (PR) values across all three classes compared to baselines.

Comparative Analysis (vs Baselines on Te.notes: CSADTL-SLD outperformed existing methods (MAFCNN-SCD, NB, KELM, MSVM, MobileNet, DenseNet-169) in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. ROC analysis indicated superior performance in distinguishing skin lesion classes.

architecture_used.main_model: CSADTL-SLD (Crow Search Algorithm with Deep Transfer Learning Driven Skin Lesion Detection)
architecture_used.pretraining: GoogleNet (Inception v1) used for feature extraction; specific pretraining weights not explicitly detailed in text, but GoogleNet is a standard pre-trained architecture.

architecture_details.backbone_structure: Three convolution layers with filter sizes 7x7, 3x3, and 1x1; nine Inception modules; global average pooling; ReLU activation; max-pooling layers after 2nd and 7th Inception modules.
architecture_details.feature_selection: Binary Crow Search Algorithm (BCSA) with sigmoid transfer function for binary feature selection (0 or 1).

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (CSA used for parameter tuning of GoogleNet).
training_details.hyperparameters: Crow Search Algorithm parameters (flight distance, awareness probability) used for tuning GoogleNet parameters; BCSA used for feature selection.

conclusions: The proposed CSADTL-SLD technique, which combines the Crow Search Algorithm (CSA) with deep transfer learning using GoogleNet for feature extraction and a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) for classification, effectively automates the detection and classification of skin lesions in dermoscopic images.
The CSADTL-SLD method demonstrated superior performance compared to existing methods (MAFCNN-SCD, NB, KELM, MSVM, MobileNet, and DenseNet-169) in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

On a dataset of 2000 samples (Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Nevus) with a 70:30 train-test split, the CSADTL-SLD model achieved an average accuracy of 94.24% and sensitivity of 84.70% on the training set, and 94.22% accuracy and 84.10% sensitivity on the testing set.
The system consistently achieved higher Precision-Recall (PR) values across all three lesion classes compared to baseline methods, indicating improved precision and recall capabilities.

Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis confirmed that the CSADTL-SLD system excels in distinguishing between different types of skin lesions, establishing its strength in enhancing diagnostic accuracy and classification.

The integration of median filtering for noise removal, GoogleNet for capturing intricate data patterns, CSA for parameter tuning, and MLP for classification presents a potent and effective approach for skin lesion detection.
important_remarks: The study uses a custom, non-public dataset of 2000 dermoscopic images (Melanoma: 374, Seborrheic Keratosis: 254, Nevus: 1372) with no specified source or external validation, limiting generalizability.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Nevus samples (1372) outnumbering Melanoma (374) and Seborrheic Keratosis (254) samples.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons rely solely on descriptive metrics.

The paper does not report data leakage prevention strategies or confirm that the 70:30 train-test split was performed at the patient level rather than the image level, risking data leakage if multiple images from the same patient were in both sets.
The source of the dermoscopic images is not disclosed, raising concerns about potential selection bias and lack of demographic diversity.
External validation on independent, multi-center datasets is absent, which is critical for assessing clinical relevance and robustness.
The model relies on a fixed 70:30 split without cross-validation, which may lead to unstable performance estimates.
The paper claims superiority over baselines (MAFCNN-SCD, MobileNet, DenseNet-169) but does not provide details on how these baselines were implemented or retrained for fair comparison.
